message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) are founded to be effective to delay epidemic spread and to reduce the number of patients. Moderate NPIs took in Japan seemed to have reduced the COVID-19 patients and to lower death rates, thus, effects of those NPIs are worthy of investigation. We used open source data and divided the data into three periods: Jan 22 to Feb 25 (Period I), Feb 26 to Apr 6 (Period II), and Apr 7 to May 14 (Period III). We developed the SIRD model and applied the Monte Carlo Simulation to estimate a combination of optimal results, including the peak of infected cases, the peak date, and
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  . For Period I, the estimated peak infected cases were smaller than the observed ones, the peak date was earlier than the observed one, and the
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  was about 4.66. For the other two periods, the estimated cases were more, and the peak dates were earlier than the observed ones. The
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  was 2.50 in Period II, and 1.79 in Period III. NPIs took in Japan might have reduced more than 50% of the daily contacts per people compared to that before COVID-19. Owing to the effects of NPIs, the Japanese society had avoided collapse of medical service. Nevertheless, the capacity of daily RT-PCR may have restricted the reported confirmed cases.
                </jats:p>
title: The effect of non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) on the spread of COVID-19 pandemic in Japan: A modeling study
